title: Psalms 92
subtitle: It Is Good to Praise the LORD

heb.paragraph: "It is good (ṭōb) to praise the LORD and make music to your name, O Most High" (v.1). A Sabbath psalm (superscription: "for the Sabbath day"). The appropriate response to God’s works: morning love, nightly faithfulness, music on harp and lyre (vv.1–3). "How great are your works, LORD, how profound your thoughts!" (v.5). The senseless do not understand (v.6); the wicked flourish briefly like grass (v.7) but are destroyed forever (v.7).

notes.note: "It is good to praise the LORD, proclaiming your love in the morning and your faithfulness at night, to the music of the ten-stringed lyre." Morning ḥesed and evening faithfulness: the rhythm of Sabbath worship.
"How great are your works! The senseless do not understand. Though the wicked spring up like grass, they will be destroyed forever." The wicked’s prosperity is temporary; God’s works are eternal.

sections.header: Verses 9–15 — “The Righteous Will Flourish Like a Palm Tree”

heb.paragraph: "The righteous will flourish like a palm tree (tāmār), they will grow like a cedar of Lebanon; planted in the house of the LORD, they will flourish in the courts of our God. They will still bear fruit in old age, they will stay fresh and green" (vv.12–14). Two trees: palm (productive, upright) and cedar (massive, enduring). The righteous are planted in God’s house — not wild but cultivated. And the extraordinary promise: fruit in old age. Freshness in the grey years.

notes.note: "The righteous will flourish like a palm tree, grow like a cedar. Planted in the house of the LORD, they will STILL BEAR FRUIT IN OLD AGE." Palm: productive beauty. Cedar: majestic endurance. Old-age fruit: the promise that relevance does not expire.

"Proclaiming, ‘The LORD is upright; he is my Rock, and there is no wickedness in him.’" The psalm’s closing confession: God is straight, solid, and without flaw. The righteous tree and the righteous God mirror each other.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: ’" The psalm’s closing confession: God is straight, solid, and without flaw…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVFor surely your enemies, Lord, surely your enemies will perish; all evildoers will be scattered.ESVFor behold, your enemies, O LORD, for behold, your enemies shall perish; all evildoers shall be scattered.NIVproclaiming your love in the morning and your faithfulness at night,ESVto declare your steadfast love in the morning, and your faithfulness by night,

rec.text: ""It is good to praise the LORD, proclaiming your love in the morning and your faithfulness at night, to the music of the ten-stringed lyre." Morning ḥesed and evening faithfulness: the rhythm of Sabba…"

rows.label: Sabbath Praise — 92:1–8
rows.text: "It is good to praise the LORD." Morning love, evening faithfulness. Great works, profound thoughts.

hebtext.note: "It is good (ṭōb) to praise the LORD and make music to your name, O Most High" (v.1). A Sabbath psalm (superscription: "for the Sabbath day"). The appropriate response to God’s works: morning love, nightly faithfulness, music on harp and lyre (vv.1–3). "How great are your works, LORD, how profound your thoughts!" (v.5). The senseless do not understand (v.6); the wicked flourish briefly like grass (v.7) but are destroyed forever (v.7).

"The righteous will flourish like a palm tree (tāmār), they will grow like a cedar of Lebanon; planted in the house of the LORD, they will flourish in the courts of our God. They will still bear fruit in old age, they will stay fresh and green" (vv.12–14). Two trees: palm (productive, upright) and cedar (massive, enduring). The righteous are planted in God’s house — not wild but cultivated. And the extraordinary promise: fruit in old age. Freshness in the grey years.

themes.note: Worship peaks: Sabbath praise. Faith: palm and cedar — fruitfulness in old age. The Sabbath psalm connects to Ps 1’s tree imagery.
